snapshot.plugin/skills/search-wikipedia/SKILL.md:
---
name: search-wikipedia
model: claude-sonnet-4-6
description: Look up a topic on Wikipedia and save the summary as a markdown file in the user's working folder. Use this when the user asks to look up, save, or research information about a topic, person, or place.
allowed-tools:
  - wikipedia_search
---

# Wikipedia Lookup

*(This skill is intentionally minimal — it's the simplified reference example for new contributors. For the richer pattern with positive triggers and negative guards, see `citation/SKILL.md` or `record-extraction/SKILL.md`.)*

**Narration:** Read `researcher_profile.narration_guidance` from `research.json` and apply it as your narration style for this invocation. If absent, default to a one-line preamble per action.

A reference skill demonstrating the full plugin pipeline: calling an
MCP tool, populating a markdown template with the result, and saving
the file to disk. Copy this structure when wiring a new skill to one
of the other MCP tools.

## What to do

When the user asks to look up a topic:

1. Call the `wikipedia_search` MCP tool with the topic as the
   `query` parameter.
2. The tool returns `{ title, extract, url }`.
3. Read the template at `templates/wiki-summary.md` (relative to
   this skill directory).
4. Fill in the template — replace `{{title}}`, `{{extract}}`, and
   `{{url}}` with the corresponding fields from the tool result.
5. Save the result as `<title-slug>.md` in the user's current
   working folder. Build `<title-slug>` from the article title by:
   - lowercasing the title;
   - replacing every run of non-alphanumeric characters (spaces, commas,
     periods, apostrophes, parentheses, etc.) with a single hyphen;
   - trimming leading/trailing hyphens.

   Examples:
   - `"Albert Einstein"` → `albert-einstein`
   - `"Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania"` → `schuylkill-county-pennsylvania`
     (the comma collapses with the surrounding space into one hyphen)
   - `"O'Brien (surname)"` → `o-brien-surname`
6. Tell the user the file was created.

## Example

User: "Look up Albert Einstein"

You should:
1. Call `wikipedia_search({ query: "Albert Einstein" })`
2. Receive `{ title: "Albert Einstein", extract: "Albert Einstein was a German-born theoretical physicist...", url: "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein" }`
3. Fill in the template
4. Write `albert-einstein.md` to the working folder
5. Tell the user the file was created

I'll look up the Wikipedia article and read the template at the same time!Now let me search Wikipedia for the United States census!Got the article! Now I'll fill in the template and save the file.

**Slug:** `united-states-census` (from "United States census" → lowercased → spaces replaced with hyphens)The file **`united-states-census.md`** has been created in your working folder! Here's a summary of what was saved:

> **United States census** — A decennial census mandated by Article I, Section 2 of the U.S. Constitution. First conducted in 1790, it has been taken every 10 years since. The Census Bureau releases underlying records 72 years later (the "72-Year Rule"). Genealogists especially rely on the 1790–1950 surveys for residence, household composition, age, occupation, and immigration origin.
>
> 🔗 [Wikipedia source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_census)

output.text_response:
I'll look up the Great Famine on Wikipedia and save the summary for you. Let me fetch the article and grab the template at the same time!Now let me search Wikipedia for the Great Famine!Got the article. Now I'll fill in the template and save it to your working folder.

- **Title slug:** `great-famine-ireland` (`"Great Famine (Ireland)"` → lowercase, non-alphanumeric runs → hyphens, trim)The Wikipedia summary has been saved to **`great-famine-ireland.md`** in your working folder. Here's what it covers:

---

### 📄 Great Famine (Ireland)

The Great Famine (also called the **Great Hunger** or **Irish Potato Famine**) was a catastrophic period of **mass starvation and disease in Ireland from 1845 to 1852**. Key facts:

- The worst-affected areas were the **west and south of Ireland** (Irish-speaking regions), known in Irish as *An Drochshaol* ("the hard times").
- It caused approximately **1 million deaths** and drove **1 million more to emigrate** — primarily to the United States, Britain, Canada, and Australia.
- Ireland's population **fell by 20–25%**, with some towns losing up to 67% of their population.

---

This is very relevant to your ancestor! An emigration **around 1850** places them squarely in the peak famine exodus period. This context could help you:

- **Narrow arrival dates** — most famine emigrants arrived in the US between 1845–1855
- **Focus on ports of entry** — New York, Boston, Philadelphia, and Quebec/New Orleans were the most common
- **Target record sets** — passenger manifests, Irish civil registration (began 1864), Griffith's Valuation (1847–1864), and Tithe Applotment Books are key sources for this era

Would you like me to pull up a **locality guide** for a specific Irish county, or help **search for emigration/arrival records** for your ancestor?

# Locality Guide: Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania (1811–Present)

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Formed** | March 1, 1811, from Berks County and Northampton County |
| **County seat** | Pottsville (platted 1816, incorporated 1828) |
| **Parent jurisdiction** | Commonwealth of Pennsylvania |
| **Population** | ~11,000 (1820) → ~60,000 (1860) → ~130,000 (1880) → ~200,000+ (1910) → ~148,000 (2020) |
| **Economy** | Anthracite coal mining (dominant 1820s–1950s); also iron manufacturing, railroading, agriculture in outlying townships |
| **Ethnic composition** | Welsh and Irish (1820s–1850s); German (1840s–1870s); Polish, Lithuanian, Ukrainian, Slovak, Italian, and other Eastern/Southern Europeans (1880s–1920s) |
| **Religious denominations** | Welsh Calvinistic Methodist, Congregationalist, Welsh Baptist; Roman Catholic (Irish and later Eastern European parishes); Lutheran; German Reformed/Evangelical; Methodist Episcopal; Episcopal; Eastern Orthodox; Jewish |
| **Key events** | Molly Maguires labor conflict (1860s–1870s, trials 1876–77); Long Strike of 1875; anthracite coal strike of 1902; decline of coal industry (1930s–1960s) |

---

## Boundary Changes

- **Stable since 1811.** Schuylkill County's boundaries have remained largely unchanged since formation. It borders Berks, Carbon, Columbia, Dauphin, Lebanon, Luzerne, and Northumberland Counties.
- **Parent jurisdiction records (pre-1811):** For events before March 1811, search **Berks County** (southern and western parts of Schuylkill) and **Northampton County** (northern and eastern portions). Berks County records date from 1752; Northampton from 1752. Both are well-documented.
- No major boundary transfers have moved townships in or out of the county since formation.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Start date:** Pennsylvania's first statewide registration attempt (1852–1854) was largely unsuccessful; compliance was poor and Schuylkill County records from that period are sparse. Reliable **birth and death registration began in 1893** (Act of 1893). **Death certificates** required statewide from **1906**. **Birth certificates** required statewide from **1906** (earlier births recorded retroactively in some cases).
- **Marriage licenses:** Issued by the county clerk from the **mid-19th century** onward; pre-license marriages evidenced in church registers and county court records.
- **What exists:**
  - Birth certificates: 1893–present (gaps in 1893–1905 compliance)
  - Death certificates: 1906–present
  - Marriage licenses: county-level from ~1852 onward; scattered earlier marriages in court minute books
- **Where held:**
  - Births/deaths 1906–1963: **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) — *indexed + images online via Ancestry and FamilySearch*
  - Births/deaths 1964–present: **Pennsylvania Department of Health**, Division of Vital Records, New Castle, PA — *physical only; certified copies by mail/in-person*
  - Marriage records: **Schuylkill County Courthouse**, Register of Wills / Clerk of Courts, Pottsville — *physical + some indexed on Ancestry*
- **Gaps:** Pre-1893 civil vital records essentially do not exist. Rely on church records for baptisms, marriages, and burials before that date.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church records (see below); family Bible records; cemetery inscriptions; newspaper notices.

---

### Church Records

The most critical substitute for pre-1893 vital records. Schuylkill County had a rich and diverse religious landscape due to successive immigration waves.

| Denomination | Language of Records | Repository |
|---|---|---|
| Welsh Calvinistic Methodist / Presbyterian | Welsh and English | FamilySearch microfilm; local churches; Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia |
| Welsh Baptist / Congregationalist | Welsh and English | FamilySearch microfilm; local churches |
| Roman Catholic (Irish parishes) | English / Latin | Philadelphia Archdiocesan Historical Research Center, Wynnewood, PA (pre-1961); Diocese of Allentown Archives (post-1961) |
| Roman Catholic (Eastern European parishes — Polish, Lithuanian, Ukrainian, Slovak) | Polish / Lithuanian / Ukrainian / Latin | Diocese of Allentown Archives, Allentown, PA; individual parish offices |
| Lutheran (German) | German and English | FamilySearch microfilm; Lutheran Archives Center at Philadelphia; individual congregations |
| German Reformed / Evangelical | German and English | FamilySearch microfilm; Evangelical and Reformed Historical Society, Lancaster; individual congregations |
| Methodist Episcopal | English | FamilySearch microfilm; Drew University Archives (NJ); local churches |
| Episcopal | English | Diocese of Bethlehem Archives, South Bethlehem |
| Eastern Orthodox (Ukrainian, Russian) | Church Slavonic | Individual parishes; OCA diocesan archives |

- **FamilySearch Catalog** lists microfilmed Schuylkill County church records by denomination. Search at [familysearch.org/catalog](https://www.familysearch.org/catalog) → Place: *Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania* → Church Records.
- **Pennsylvania Church and Town Records** collection on **Ancestry** includes many Schuylkill County congregational registers.
- Many Welsh-language records require translation; German-language records from Lutheran and Reformed churches may use Gothic (Kurrent) script.

---

### Census Records

| Census | Years Available | Access Level |
|---|---|---|
| U.S. Federal Census | 1820, 1830, 1840, 1850, 1860, 1870, 1880, 1900, 1910, 1920, 1930, 1940, 1950 | **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch (free) and Ancestry (subscription) |
| 1890 Federal Census | Largely destroyed by fire and water (1921) | **Destroyed** — see substitute sources below |
| Federal Mortality Schedules | 1850, 1860, 1870, 1880 | **Indexed + images** — Ancestry; FamilySearch |
| Federal Agricultural / Industrial Schedules | 1850–1880 | **Browse-only images** — Ancestry; FamilySearch |
| Federal Veterans' Schedule | 1890 (Union veterans) | **Indexed + images** — Ancestry |
| Pennsylvania State Census | Pennsylvania did not conduct systematic state censuses | N/A |
| Taxable Inhabitant Lists | 1800s onward at county level | **Physical / microfilm** — Pennsylvania State Archives; some on Ancestry |

- **1890 substitute sources:** 1890 veterans' schedule (survives); 1880 census + 1900 census to bracket the decade; city directories; tax records; church registers.

---

### Probate and Court Records

- **Types:** Wills, letters of administration, guardianship bonds, inventories of estate, account settlements; also name changes, indentures, civil suits.
- **Date range:** 1811–present (from county formation); earlier estates involving Schuylkill area residents in **Berks County** (Orphans' Court records, Reading) and **Northampton County** (Easton).
- **Where held:**
  - **Register of Wills / Orphans' Court**, Schuylkill County Courthouse, Pottsville — *physical; some indices on FamilySearch and Ancestry*
  - **Pennsylvania Wills and Probate Records, 1683–1993** on **Ancestry** — *indexed + images*; includes Schuylkill County
  - **FamilySearch** — Pennsylvania Probate Records collection includes Schuylkill County wills and administrations
- **Access:** ✅ Well-covered online. Ancestry's Pennsylvania wills collection is particularly strong for 19th-century records.

---

### Land Records

- **System:** Pennsylvania is a **state-land state** (not a federal public land state). Land was originally granted through the Penn proprietorship and later the Commonwealth via warrants, surveys, and patents.
- **Types:**
  - *Original grants:* Land warrants, surveys, and patents — **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Land Records Section, Harrisburg *(indexed + images online via PA State Archives and Ancestry's Pennsylvania Land Records)*
  - *Subsequent transfers:* Deed books — **Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds**, Pottsville, from 1811 *(physical; some early deed books on FamilySearch microfilm)*
  - *Tax assessment records:* County assessment books, 1811–present — **Schuylkill County Assessment Office** / **Pennsylvania State Archives**
- **Useful finding aids:**
  - FamilySearch Catalog: *Pennsylvania Land Records* collection
  - Ancestry: *Pennsylvania, Land Records, 1634–1831* (pre-county formation warrants)

---

### Military Records

| Conflict | Record Types | Repository / Access |
|---|---|---|
| War of 1812 | PA militia service records, pension applications | Pennsylvania State Archives; NARA (digitized on Fold3 / Ancestry) |
| Mexican War (1846–1848) | PA volunteer muster rolls | Pennsylvania State Archives; NARA |
| Civil War (1861–1865) | PA regimental service records, pension files, bounty records | Pennsylvania State Archives; NARA; Fold3 (indexed); Ancestry |
| Spanish-American War (1898) | PA volunteer records | Pennsylvania State Archives |
| World War I | Draft registration cards (Selective Service) | NARA Atlanta; **Ancestry** (indexed + images); **FamilySearch** |
| World War II | Draft registration (WWII "Old Man's Draft"); enlistment records | NARA; **Ancestry** (indexed + images) |
| Veterans' schedules | 1890 Union veterans & widows | **Ancestry** (indexed + images) |

- **Pennsylvania State Archives** holds an extensive collection of Civil War-era records, including muster rolls for Schuylkill County regiments (notably the 96th PA Infantry and 48th PA Infantry, both heavily recruited from the county's coal regions).
- **Grand Army of the Republic (GAR)** post records for Schuylkill County may be at the Historical Society of Schuylkill County or Pennsylvania State Archives.

---

### Cemetery Records

- **Online coverage:**
  - **FindAGrave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)) — extensive Schuylkill County coverage, including many ethnic and religious cemeteries. **Indexed + images** (free).
  - **BillionGraves** — supplemental coverage; includes GPS-tagged headstone photos.
- **Notable cemeteries:** Charles Baber Cemetery (Pottsville, est. 1825); Odd Fellows Cemetery; Presbyterian Cemetery; St. Patrick's Cemetery; numerous township and denominational burying grounds.
- **Ethnic cemeteries:** Many Eastern European immigrant communities (Polish, Lithuanian, Ukrainian) maintained their own parish cemeteries, sometimes with records only in the heritage language.
- **Physical sexton records:** Held by individual cemeteries or the Historical Society of Schuylkill County. Not universally digitized.

---

### Newspaper Records

- **The Miners' Journal** (Pottsville, 1825–present) — one of the **oldest continuously published newspapers in the United States**. Invaluable for obituaries, marriage notices, legal notices (estate filings, land sales), and mining accident reports.
  - Early issues (1825–1922): **Chronicling America** ([chroniclingamerica.loc.gov](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov)) — *free, full-text searchable*
  - Later issues: **State Library of Pennsylvania** microfilm; **Newspapers.com** (subscription)
- **Other county papers:** Pottsville Republican; Tamaqua Courier; Shenandoah Herald; Mahanoy City Record-American; various ethnic-language papers (Polish, Lithuanian, Ukrainian)
  - Many held on microfilm at the **State Library of Pennsylvania**, Harrisburg
  - Some on **Newspapers.com** and **GenealogyBank** (subscriptions)
- **Research value:** Obituaries in the Miners' Journal frequently name survivors, birthplace, and length of residence — critical for pre-1893 vital data.

---

### Immigration and Naturalization Records

Given Schuylkill County's heavy 19th–20th century immigration, these records are especially important.

- **Ports of entry:** Philadelphia (primary for Welsh, Irish, and early German immigrants); New York/Castle Garden (pre-1892) and Ellis Island (1892–1954) for later waves.
- **Naturalization records:**
  - Filed at **Schuylkill County Court of Common Pleas**, Pottsville (declarations of intent + petitions for naturalization, from ~1840s onward)
  - Also filed in federal courts
  - **Pennsylvania Naturalization Records** on **Ancestry** (indexed + images) — strong coverage
  - **FamilySearch** — Pennsylvania Naturalization and Citizenship Records
  - **NARA Philadelphia** holds federal court naturalization records
- **Passenger lists:**
  - **Ancestry** (indexed + images): Castle Garden and Ellis Island arrivals; Philadelphia passenger lists
  - **FamilySearch** (indexed + images): U.S. Arriving Passenger and Crew Lists
  - **Ellis Island Foundation** ([libertyellisfoundation.org](https://www.libertyellisfoundation.org)) — free Ellis Island search
- **Note on Eastern European immigrants:** Many Poles, Lithuanians, and Ukrainians naturalized under different or anglicized spellings. Search variants (e.g., "Kowalski" / "Kowalske"; "Petrenko" / "Petrenco").

---

### Tax Records

- **Types:** County assessment rolls (taxable inhabitants, real estate, personal property), 1811–present; earlier records for Schuylkill area in Berks and Northampton County assessment rolls.
- **Where held:** **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg; **Schuylkill County Assessment Office**
- **Access:** Microfilm at Pennsylvania State Archives; some records on Ancestry's *Pennsylvania Tax Lists* collection.
- **Substitute value:** Tax lists for 1820–1840 (before comprehensive census schedules) and 1890 (destroyed census year) can confirm presence and land ownership.

---

### Occupational Records (Coal Industry)

This is a **uniquely important** record category for Schuylkill County that most general guides overlook.

- **Pennsylvania Bureau of Mines annual reports** (1870–1930s): List mining accident victims by name, mine, and date. Searchable at **HathiTrust** and **Internet Archive**. *Free, full-text searchable.*
- **Mine employment records:** Philadelphia and Reading Coal and Iron Company; Lehigh Coal and Navigation Company; various colliery operators. Surviving records at **Hagley Museum and Library**, Wilmington, DE; **Pennsylvania State Archives**; **Historical Society of Schuylkill County**.
- **Miners' union records:** United Mine Workers of America (UMWA) records — some held at **Penn State University Special Collections** (University Park, PA).
- **Molly Maguires trial records (1876–1877):** Schuylkill County Court records; extensively published; available at the Historical Society of Schuylkill County and in printed form.

---

### City and County Directories

- **Pottsville and Schuylkill County directories** published from the **1840s** onward; list heads of household with address and occupation.
- **Where held:** **Historical Society of Schuylkill County**; **State Library of Pennsylvania**; **Ancestry** (*U.S. City Directories* collection — subscription, indexed)

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Key Collections for Schuylkill County | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** ([familysearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org)) | PA vital records (births 1893–1913, deaths 1906–1967); U.S. Census all years; PA church records; PA probate records; PA naturalization; WWI draft cards | **Free** |
| **Ancestry** ([ancestry.com](https://www.ancestry.com)) | PA birth/death certificates 1906–1968; all federal censuses; PA wills & probate 1683–1993; PA naturalization; PA church & town records; city directories; Fold3 military records | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** ([chroniclingamerica.loc.gov](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov)) | *Miners' Journal* 1825–1922; other PA papers | **Free** |
| **Newspapers.com** | *Miners' Journal* and other Schuylkill County papers (later years) | Subscription |
| **GenealogyBank** | Historical newspapers including PA titles | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)) | Schuylkill County cemetery transcriptions and headstone photos | **Free** |
| **Fold3** ([fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com)) | Civil War and other military service/pension records | Subscription |
| **HathiTrust / Internet Archive** | Pennsylvania Bureau of Mines annual reports (mine accident victims) | **Free** |
| **PA State Archives online** ([digitalarchives.state.pa.us](https://digitalarchives.state.pa.us)) | Land records, some military, some vital records | **Free** |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Schuylkill County Courthouse**, 401 N. 2nd St., Pottsville, PA 17901 | Deeds (Recorder of Deeds), wills & estates (Register of Wills), marriage licenses, court records, naturalization records | In-person; limited mail requests |
| **Historical Society of Schuylkill County**, 305 N. Centre St., Pottsville, PA 17901 | Local history, genealogical files, city directories, cemetery records, some church records, photographs, Molly Maguires materials | In-person; contact for hours: (570) 622-7540 |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives**, 350 North St., Harrisburg, PA 17120 | Land warrants/patents, state vital records, military records, tax assessment records, naturalization records, state government records | In-person; some records online; email/mail requests |
| **State Library of Pennsylvania**, 333 Market St., Harrisburg, PA 17126 | Newspaper microfilm (incl. *Miners' Journal*), PA genealogy books, county histories | In-person; interlibrary loan for microfilm |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania**, 1300 Locust St., Philadelphia, PA 19107 | Manuscript collections, some church records, Philadelphia-area newspapers, PA genealogy | In-person; subscription access for some online resources |
| **Philadelphia Archdiocesan Historical Research Center**, 100 E. Wynnewood Rd., Wynnewood, PA 19096 | Catholic parish registers (pre-1961 Schuylkill County parishes — Diocese of Allentown created 1961) | In-person by appointment |
| **Diocese of Allentown Archives**, PO Box F, Allentown, PA 18105 | Catholic parish records for Schuylkill County (Diocese of Allentown post-1961) | Contact for access |
| **Lutheran Archives Center at Philadelphia**, 7301 Germantown Ave., Philadelphia, PA 19119 | Pennsylvania Lutheran church registers | In-person by appointment |
| **Evangelical and Reformed Historical Society**, 555 W. James St., Lancaster, PA 17603 | German Reformed / Evangelical church records | In-person; some finding aids online |
| **Hagley Museum and Library**, 298 Buck Rd., Wilmington, DE 19807 | Coal and iron company employment/business records | In-person; some finding aids online |
| **Penn State University Special Collections**, University Park, PA 16802 | UMWA records, coal industry archives | In-person; finding aids online |
| **NARA Philadelphia**, 900 Market St., Philadelphia, PA 19107 | Federal court naturalization records; federal agency records | In-person; some records on Ancestry/Fold3 |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following records exist but are **not** available in any online database — do not assume they don't exist simply because they don't appear in a web search:

- **Most marriage records before 1900** (county courthouse; not fully digitized)
- **Schuylkill County deed books** (Recorder of Deeds — physical only; FamilySearch microfilm coverage is partial)
- **County tax assessment books** (Pennsylvania State Archives; partial Ancestry coverage only)
- **Coal company employment records** (Hagley Museum; Penn State; Historical Society of Schuylkill County — unindexed manuscript collections)
- **Most Eastern Orthodox, Ukrainian Catholic, and Eastern European Catholic parish registers** (held by individual parishes or diocesan archives; rarely microfilmed)
- **Sexton records for many individual cemeteries** (held by cemetery offices or the Historical Society of Schuylkill County)
- **City and county directory volumes not yet on Ancestry** (State Library of Pennsylvania; Historical Society of Schuylkill County)
- **UMWA and labor union membership records** (Penn State Special Collections — physical, finding aids online)
- **Hospital and institutional records** (Pottsville Hospital and other facilities — contact directly)

---

## Known Record Losses

✅ **Good news:** The Schuylkill County Courthouse in Pottsville **did not suffer a major courthouse fire**, unlike many Pennsylvania counties. Core records (deeds, wills, court minutes) survive from county formation in 1811.

⚠️ **Known gaps and losses:**

| Loss | Details | Substitute |
|---|---|---|
| **1890 Federal Census** | Nationally destroyed (fire, 1921; water damage) | 1890 Veterans' Schedule; 1880 + 1900 census; tax records; city directories |
| **Pre-1893 civil vital records** | Pennsylvania did not reliably register births/deaths before 1893 | Church records (baptism, burial registers); cemetery inscriptions; Miners' Journal notices |
| **1852–1854 PA registration attempt** | Sparse compliance; most Schuylkill County records from this period missing | As above |
| **Some early church records** | Not all congregations preserved 19th-century registers; some in private hands or lost | Denominational archives; cemetery records; newspaper death notices |
| **Individual coal company records** | Many small colliery operators' records lost when companies dissolved | Bureau of Mines reports; newspaper accident accounts |

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the Miners' Journal.** For any ancestor in the county from 1825 onward, this newspaper is a primary source. Obituaries often name birthplace, date of arrival, survivors, and church affiliation. Use *Chronicling America* for free full-text searching of pre-1923 issues.

2. **Identify the ethnic community first.** Schuylkill County had over a dozen distinct immigrant groups. Knowing your ancestor was Welsh, Irish, Polish, or Lithuanian tells you which churches, which language records, and which naturalization patterns to expect.

3. **Church records are your vital records before 1893.** Civil registration is essentially absent before that date. Use the FamilySearch Catalog (search by place: *Schuylkill, Pennsylvania*) to find which church records have been microfilmed.

4. **Check parent counties for pre-1811 records.** Any event before March 1, 1811 will be in **Berks County** (southeastern Schuylkill) or **Northampton County** (northeastern Schuylkill) records. Berks County has excellent pre-1811 coverage.

5. **Don't skip coal industry records.** The Bureau of Mines annual reports are free, full-text searchable at HathiTrust and often name individual miners killed in accidents by name, age, and sometimes nationality. This is a uniquely local resource with no equivalent in agricultural counties.

6. **Naturalization was often filed in county court.** Most pre-1906 naturalizations in Schuylkill County are at the county courthouse, not federal court. Ancestry's *Pennsylvania Naturalization Records* collection is the starting point; then verify originals at the courthouse.

7. **Watch for name anglicization.** Welsh names (Llewellyn, Rhys, Griffith) were often anglicized; Eastern European surnames were frequently misspelled, shortened, or phonetically re-spelled by English-speaking clerks. Search multiple variant spellings.

8. **Consult the FamilySearch Research Wiki.** The article *Pennsylvania Genealogy* and the subordinate article *Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania Genealogy* give a current list of what FamilySearch has digitized and indexed for this county. Visit: [familysearch.org/wiki/en/Schuylkill_County,_Pennsylvania_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/wiki/en/Schuylkill_County,_Pennsylvania_Genealogy)

9. **Contact the Historical Society of Schuylkill County.** They hold unique local materials—including some genealogical surname files, photographs, and coal company records—that are not online anywhere. Their staff are experienced with the county's multi-ethnic genealogy.

10. **For Catholic ancestors**, determine which diocese: **Archdiocese of Philadelphia** held the county until 1961; the **Diocese of Allentown** has held it since then. Earlier records may still be at the Archdiocesan Research Center in Wynnewood; later records at the Diocese of Allentown.

---

*Note: The FamilySearch wiki tools were unavailable during preparation of this guide. Verify current collection availability and digitization status directly at [familysearch.org/wiki](https://www.familysearch.org/wiki) and [familysearch.org/catalog](https://www.familysearch.org/catalog), as new collections are added regularly.*

output.text_response:
Here's a Python function that parses a CSV file using the built-in `csv` module:

```python
import csv
from pathlib import Path


def parse_csv(file_path: str, delimiter: str = ",", has_header: bool = True) -> list[dict | list]:
    """
    Parses a CSV file and returns its contents.

    Args:
        file_path:  Path to the CSV file.
        delimiter:  Column delimiter (default: ',').
        has_header: If True, returns a list of dicts (column name → value).
                    If False, returns a list of lists (raw rows).

    Returns:
        List of dicts (with header) or list of lists (without header).

    Raises:
        FileNotFoundError: If the file does not exist.
        ValueError:        If the file is empty.
    """
    path = Path(file_path)

    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"CSV file not found: {file_path}")

    rows = []

    with path.open(newline="", encoding="utf-8") as csv_file:
        if has_header:
            reader = csv.DictReader(csv_file, delimiter=delimiter)
            for row in reader:
                rows.append(dict(row))
        else:
            reader = csv.reader(csv_file, delimiter=delimiter)
            for row in reader:
                rows.append(row)

    if not rows:
        raise ValueError(f"CSV file is empty: {file_path}")

    return rows
```

---

### Usage Examples

**With a header row** (returns a list of dicts):
```python
# data.csv:
# name,age,city
# Alice,30,New York
# Bob,25,Los Angeles

rows = parse_csv("data.csv")
print(rows)
# [{'name': 'Alice', 'age': '30', 'city': 'New York'},
#  {'name': 'Bob',   'age': '25', 'city': 'Los Angeles'}]

# Access individual fields naturally
for row in rows:
    print(f"{row['name']} is {row['age']} years old.")
```

**Without a header row** (returns a list of lists):
```python
rows = parse_csv("data.csv", has_header=False)
# [['name', 'age', 'city'], ['Alice', '30', 'New York'], ...]
```

**Custom delimiter** (e.g. TSV):
```python
rows = parse_csv("data.tsv", delimiter="\t")
```

---

### Key design choices

| Feature | Why |
|---|---|
| `csv.DictReader` | Maps column names automatically — no index juggling |
| `pathlib.Path` | Cleaner cross-platform file handling than raw strings |
| `newline=""` | Required by the `csv` module to handle line endings correctly |
| `encoding="utf-8"` | Safe default; change to `"latin-1"` for older files if needed |
| Explicit error raises | Fail fast with a clear message instead of silently returning `[]` |

Let me know if you'd like to extend this — for example, with **type coercion** (auto-converting numbers), **streaming large files**, or **writing back to CSV**.

# Albert Einstein

Albert Einstein (14 March 1879 – 18 April 1955) was a German-born theoretical physicist who is widely held to be one of the greatest and most influential scientists of all time. Best known for developing the theory of relativity, Einstein also made important contributions to quantum mechanics. He emigrated from Germany to the United States in 1933 and became a U.S. citizen in 1940.

---
[Source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein)

### Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania

Schuylkill County is a county in the U.S. state of Pennsylvania. It was a major center of **anthracite coal mining** during the 19th and early 20th centuries, earning the region the nickname **"Coal Country."** The county seat is **Pottsville**, and the area attracted waves of Irish, Welsh, and Eastern European immigrants who worked in the mines.

🔗 [Wikipedia article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schuylkill_County,_Pennsylvania)

### O'Brien (surname)

**O'Brien** is an Irish surname — the anglicised form of the Gaelic **Ó Briain**, meaning *"descendant of Brian."* It is most famously associated with **Brian Boru**, the High King of Ireland who died at the **Battle of Clontarf in 1014**. It is one of the most common surnames in Ireland and is also widespread across the Irish diaspora in the **United States, Australia, and Canada**.

🔗 [Wikipedia: O'Brien (surname)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/O%27Brien_(surname))
